howItWorks.intro: This calculator compares the true total cost of offshore manufacturing against domestic production by accounting for all costs — not just the unit price on the purchase order.
rules: Offshore unit costs are only 20-40% of the true total cost — shipping, tariffs, quality, and overhead add significantly
Hidden offshore costs include: shipping (8-15%), tariffs (0-60%), quality defects (3-8%), inventory carrying (5-12%), IP risk (1-5%), and travel/oversight (2-5%)
Domestic benefits include: shorter lead times, lower inventory needs, easier quality control, no tariff exposure, and reduced IP theft risk
Break-even tariff rate shows at what tariff level reshoring becomes cost-neutral
howItWorks.outro: The Reshoring Initiative estimates that 20-30% of currently offshored manufacturing would be cost-competitive domestically when all hidden costs are properly accounted for. Recent tariff increases have pushed this figure even higher.

faq.question: What are the hidden costs of offshore manufacturing?
faq.answer: The biggest hidden costs include: freight and logistics (8-15% of product cost), tariffs and duties (variable), quality defect rates and rework (3-8%), excess inventory from long lead times (5-12% carrying cost), intellectual property theft risk (1-5% estimated), management travel and oversight (2-5%), and supply chain disruption risk. The Reshoring Initiative estimates total hidden costs add 20-40% to the quoted offshore price.

faq.question: Is reshoring actually happening?
faq.answer: Yes, significantly. The Reshoring Initiative reports that reshoring and foreign direct investment announcements brought back over 350,000 US manufacturing jobs in 2023 alone. Major drivers include tariff uncertainty, supply chain disruptions experienced during COVID, rising overseas labor costs, automation making US production more competitive, and government incentives like the CHIPS Act and IRA.

faq.question: How do tariffs affect the reshoring calculation?
faq.answer: Tariffs directly increase the landed cost of imported goods. A 25% tariff on a $10 offshore unit cost adds $2.50, bringing it to $12.50 before other costs. For many product categories, tariff rates above 15-30% make domestic production cost-competitive. The recent tariff increases on Chinese goods (up to 60% on some categories) have dramatically shifted the calculation.

faq.question: What about nearshoring to Mexico instead?
faq.answer: Mexico offers a middle ground: lower labor costs than the US but higher than China, minimal shipping costs and lead times, USMCA trade agreement benefits, similar time zones, and easier quality oversight. For many companies, Mexico is the pragmatic first step before full US reshoring. The calculator can model this by using Mexico as the source country.

faq.question: Is the Reshoring Cost Calculator free to use?
faq.answer: Yes, completely free with no signup required. Use it as many times as you need — there are no limits or hidden fees.

sections.title: What Is the Reshoring Cost Calculator?
sections.body: The Reshoring Cost Calculator is a free online tool designed for users who need quick, accurate calculations in the practical calculation space. By entering your product type, current offshore unit cost, annual production volume, you get instant results including offshore total / unit, domestic total / unit, cost gap. No formulas to memorize, no spreadsheets to build — just enter your numbers and get the answer in seconds. Whether you're a beginner or experienced professional, this calculator saves you time and eliminates guesswork.

sections.title: Why This Calculation Matters
sections.body: Getting offshore total / unit right can make the difference between success and costly mistakes. In practical calculation, small errors compound quickly. Manual calculations are error-prone and time-consuming, especially under pressure. This calculator applies proven formulas used by users worldwide, giving you confidence that your numbers are correct. Use it to get accurate results with precision and avoid common pitfalls that trip up beginners.

sections.title: When Should You Use This Calculator?
sections.body: This tool is most useful when you know your product type and need to find the right offshore total / unit. It's also great for quick estimates before committing to a decision, and to double-check manual calculations or professional quotes, and when comparing different scenarios side by side. Bookmark this page and come back whenever you need a fast, reliable answer — the calculator is always free and requires no signup.

howToSteps.title: Enter Your Product Type
howToSteps.description: Start by entering your product type — this is the primary input for the calculation.

howToSteps.title: Fill In Additional Details
howToSteps.description: Complete the remaining fields: current offshore unit cost, annual production volume, current source country, current/expected tariff rate. Each value refines the calculation for greater accuracy.

howToSteps.title: Click Calculate
howToSteps.description: Hit the Calculate button to run the numbers. Results appear instantly below.

howToSteps.title: Review Your Results
howToSteps.description: Check your offshore total / unit, domestic total / unit, cost gap. Use these figures to inform your next decision or compare against alternative scenarios.

tips: Double-check your product type before calculating — even small input errors can significantly change your results.
Run the calculator with different values to compare scenarios and find the optimal approach for your situation.
Pay attention to both offshore total / unit and domestic total / unit — they work together to give you the full picture.
Bookmark this page for quick access next time you need to get accurate results.
If you're unsure about your current/expected tariff rate, start with a conservative estimate and adjust from there.
